definition: A temporary hypothesis introduced during a proof, written with the `(assume F)` command. Assumptions must be discharged before the proof is complete — for example, $\to$-intro discharges an assumption by turning it into the antecedent of an implication.
intuitive: Like saying "let's pretend this is true for a moment." You borrow a fact temporarily, use it, then give it back before the proof ends.

definition: The simplest kind of formula — a single letter with no internal structure. Uppercase letters (P, Q, A…) are propositional atoms; lowercase letters (x, y, t…) are term atoms. For example, `P` and `x` are atoms; `(-> P Q)` is not.
intuitive: A single letter. The simplest possible building block of logic — like a single brick before you build a wall.

definition: A variable that appears in the scope of a quantifier ($\forall$x. … or $\exists$x. …). The quantifier 'captures' the variable, so its name can be changed (alpha-renaming) without changing the meaning of the formula.
intuitive: A variable that belongs to a quantifier. Think of it like "x" in "for every person x" — the x is just a placeholder; you could replace it with y and it would mean the same thing.

definition: The formula you are trying to prove — also called the goal. A proof is complete when the final step's conclusion matches the goal and its context contains only the allowed premises.
intuitive: The finish line. The statement you're trying to prove — if your proof were a race, the conclusion is the finish tape.

definition: The logical 'and'. A conjunction `(and p q)` is true exactly when both p and q are true. To prove a conjunction, use $\land$-intro (prove each half separately). To use one, apply $\land$-elim-l or $\land$-elim-r to extract the left or right half.
intuitive: "Both things are true at the same time." Like saying "it's raining AND I have an umbrella" — the whole statement is only true if both parts are true.

definition: A logical operator that combines smaller formulas into larger ones. The standard connectives are: $\to$ (implication), $\land$ (conjunction), $\lor$ (disjunction), $\lnot$ (negation), $\forall$ (universal quantifier), and $\exists$ (existential quantifier). Each connective has introduction and elimination rules.
intuitive: A glue word for logic. Just like "and", "or", "if…then", and "not" connect ideas in everyday speech, connectives connect formulas in logic.

definition: The set of assumptions available at a given proof step. Written as $\Gamma$ (capital Gamma). Each step in a proof carries its own context — the formulas you are allowed to rely on at that point. The $\vdash$ symbol separates the context from the conclusion.
intuitive: Your backpack of known facts. At any point in a proof, the context is what you've collected and can use — assumptions you've made, premises you started with.

definition: The always-false formula, written $\bot$ or 'bottom'. Deriving $\bot$ from an assumption lets you conclude the negation of that assumption via $\lnot$-intro. From $\bot$ alone, any formula follows — this is the principle of explosion (ex falso).
intuitive: An impossible situation. Like saying "it's raining and it's not raining" at the same time — this can never be true. In logic, reaching a contradiction means something went wrong with your assumptions.

definition: The logical 'or'. A disjunction `(or p q)` is true when at least one of p or q is true. To prove one, use $\lor$-intro-l or $\lor$-intro-r (pick one side and prove it). To use a disjunction, apply $\lor$-elim (proof by cases): show that the goal follows from either possibility.
intuitive: "At least one of these is true (maybe both)." When you say "I'll have tea OR coffee," in everyday English you usually mean one or the other. In logic, $\lor$ means at least one is true — both being true is fine too.

definition: To remove an assumption from the context, closing off the subproof that depended on it. Introduction rules (like $\to$-intro, $\lnot$-intro, $\forall$-intro, $\exists$-elim) discharge assumptions: $\to$-intro discharges a propositional assumption by turning it into the antecedent of an implication; $\lnot$-intro discharges by concluding its negation; $\forall$-intro and $\exists$-elim discharge variable assumptions. A proof is complete when all assumptions have been discharged — only premises may remain.
intuitive: Returning what you borrowed. You temporarily assume something, use it, then give it back before the proof ends. Think of it like checking out a library book — you can use it while you have it, but you must return it before you leave.

definition: The formula $\lnot \lnot p$ ('not not p'). In classical logic, $\lnot \lnot p$ is equivalent to p, and you can eliminate the double negation with the `(dne N)` command. This rule is not valid in intuitionistic logic.
intuitive: "Not not" cancels out. Saying "I'm not unhappy" means you're happy. Two negatives make a positive — that's exactly what double negation elimination does.

definition: A rule for using (taking apart) a formula whose main connective is the one being eliminated. Example: from $p \to q$ and p, use $\to$-elim (modus ponens) to derive q. Elimination rules let you extract information from complex formulas.
intuitive: A rule for taking apart and using something you already proved. If you've proved "A and B", the elimination rule lets you say "so A is true." You're unpacking what you already have.

definition: The principle of explosion: from $\bot$ (a contradiction), any formula whatsoever follows. The name is Latin for 'from falsehood, anything'. Use the `(ex-falso N F)` command — step N must prove $\bot$, and F is the formula you want to derive.
intuitive: From nonsense, anything follows. If you've accidentally proved something impossible ($\bot$), you can conclude literally any statement — because in a broken system, anything goes. The name is Latin for "from falsehood."

definition: The $\exists$-intro rule: if you have proved $\varphi$ with some specific term t in place of x, you can conclude $\exists$x.$\varphi$. 'If a concrete example exists, then something exists.' Use the `(exists-intro N t x)` command.
intuitive: "I found one, so at least one exists." If you can point to a specific example (say, a red apple), then you can conclude "there exists something red."

definition: Written $\exists$ and typed as `exists`. The formula $\exists$x.$\varphi$ reads 'there exists an x such that $\varphi$'. To prove it, use $\exists$-intro (provide a witness). To use it, apply $\exists$-elim (introduce a fresh witness variable, prove your goal, then discharge the witness).
intuitive: "There exists" or "there is at least one." Like saying "someone in this room speaks French" — you're claiming at least one person does, without necessarily saying who.

definition: A well-formed expression in the logical language. Atoms (single letters like P or x) are formulas, and larger formulas are built by combining smaller ones with connectives: `(not p)`, `(-> p q)`, `(and p q)`, `(or p q)`, `(forall x p)`, `(exists x p)`, and `(App P t)`.
intuitive: A grammatically correct sentence in logic. Just like "the cat sat on the mat" is a valid English sentence, `(-> P Q)` is a valid formula. A random jumble of symbols is not.

definition: A variable that is not bound by any quantifier in a formula. In $\forall$-intro, the variable being generalised must not appear free in any other assumption — otherwise the generalisation is unsound (you would be claiming 'for all x' when you only proved it for a specific x).
intuitive: A variable that's "on its own" — not owned by any quantifier. If you say "x is tall" without specifying which x, that's a free variable. It's like using a pronoun without saying who you mean.

definition: A mathematical pair $\langle W, R \rangle$ where $W$ is a non-empty set of possible worlds and $R \subseteq W \times W$ is a binary accessibility relation between those worlds. It provides the geometric structure for modal logic semantics without assigning truth values to atomic propositions.
intuitive: A map of realities. The worlds are the cities (nodes), and the accessibility relation provides the roads (directed edges) connecting them, defining where you can travel from where.

definition: The logical 'if…then'. `(-> p q)` means 'p implies q' or 'if p then q'. To prove an implication, use $\to$-intro: temporarily assume p, derive q, then discharge the assumption. To use one, apply $\to$-elim (modus ponens): from $p \to q$ and p, conclude q.
intuitive: "If this, then that." The arro$w \to m$eans if the left side is true, the right side must also be true. Not causation — just a promise: whenever p happens, q follows.

definition: A rule that derives a new judgement from one or more existing judgements. Written as a fraction: premises above the line, conclusion below. Each command in the REPL (like `(->-intro F N)`) corresponds to applying one inference rule.
intuitive: A step in the proof game. Each rule tells you: if you already have these things (above the line), you may now claim this new thing (below the line).

definition: A rule for proving a formula whose main connective is the one being introduced. Example: to prove $p \to q$, use $\to$-intro — assume p, derive q, then discharge. Each connective has its own introduction rule.
intuitive: A rule for building and proving something new. If you want to prove "A and B", the introduction rule says: prove A, prove B separately, then put them together. You're constructing, not unpacking.

definition: The assertion that formula p follows from the assumptions in context $\Gamma$. The $\vdash$ symbol is called a 'turnstile' and reads as 'proves' or 'entails'. Every step in a natural deduction proof is a judgement.
intuitive: A claim backed by evidence. Writing "$\Gamma \vdash$ p" is like saying "from what I know ($\Gamma$), I can conclude p." The turnstile $\vdash$ is the word "therefore" — it separates your evidence from your conclusion.

definition: The $\to$-elim rule: if you have proved $p \to q$ and you have proved p, you may conclude q. One of the oldest and most fundamental rules of logic. The name is Latin for 'mode that affirms'.
intuitive: The most natural reasoning step: if you know "if A then B" and you know A is true, then B must be true. Like: "if it rains, the ground gets wet" + "it's raining" $\to$ "the ground is wet."

definition: A proof system where each logical connective has introduction rules (how to prove a statement with that connective) and elimination rules (how to use a statement with that connective). Proofs are built step-by-step by applying these rules, working forward from assumptions toward the goal.
intuitive: A way of doing proofs that mimics how people actually think. You introduce assumptions when needed, work forward, and discharge them at the end. No complicated machinery — just step-by-step reasoning.

definition: The logical 'not'. `(not p)` is true exactly when p is false. To prove $\lnot p$, use $\lnot$-intro (assume p, derive a contradiction). To use $\lnot p$, apply $\lnot$-elim to convert it to $p $\to \bot$. You can also eliminate double negations with `(dne N)`.
intuitive: "Not." The $\lnot s$ymbol flips the truth value: if something was true, $\lnot m$akes it false, and vice versa. Like putting "it is not the case that" in front of a statement.

definition: A symbol (uppercase letter) that represents a property or relation. A predicate applied to a term, written `(App P x)`, forms a formula — for example, `(App Human Socrates)` means 'Socrates is human'. Predicates can take multiple arguments via nested application: `(App (App Loves x) y)`.
intuitive: A property or relation. "Is tall" is a predicate — it's incomplete until you fill in who is tall. In `(App Tall x)`, `Tall` is the predicate and `x` is who we're talking about.

definition: A formula given as true at the start of a proof. Premises appear in the initial context and can be used without justification. The goal is to derive the conclusion from the premises alone.
intuitive: A starting fact you're given for free. Like the clues in a mystery novel — you don't need to prove them; they're the raw material you work with to reach the conclusion.

definition: A statement that is either true or false. In propositional logic, uppercase letters (P, Q, R…) stand for arbitrary propositions. Connectives combine propositions into more complex ones.
intuitive: A statement that is either true or false — no ambiguity, no "maybe." "The sky is blue" is a proposition (it's either true or false). "Hello!" is not — it's not claiming anything.

definition: A symbol that specifies how many things a statement applies to. The two quantifiers in first-order logic are $\forall$ (universal, 'for all') and $\exists$ (existential, 'there exists'). Quantifiers bind variables, turning an open formula like 'x is human' into a closed statement like 'everything is human' or 'something is human'.
intuitive: A word that says how many things we're talking about. $\forall$ means "all of them" and $\exists$ means "at least one." Without quantifiers, logic can only talk about specific named things.

definition: Proof by contradiction via $\lnot$-intro: assume p, derive both q and $\lnot q$ (a contradiction), then conclude $\lnot p$. The name is Latin for 'reduction to absurdity'. This is the standard way to prove a negation.
intuitive: Proof by showing the opposite would be ridiculous. Assume the opposite of what you want to prove, find that it leads to nonsense (a contradiction), and conclude your original statement must be true. Latin for "reduction to absurdity."

definition: Short for 'symbolic expression'. A notation where everything is written in parentheses with the operator first: `(and p q)` instead of `$p \land q$`. Plato uses s-expressions for both formulas and commands. For example, '$p \to q$' is written `(-> p q)`, and the modus ponens command is `(->-elim N M)`.
intuitive: A way of writing where the operator always comes first in parentheses. Instead of "A + B" you write "(+ A B)". It looks odd at first but it's completely unambiguous — no need for order-of-operations rules.

definition: Replacing an atom with a formula throughout a proof step. The `(subst N (A F)...)` command substitutes atom A with formula F uniformly in step N. Useful for instantiating schematic patterns with concrete formulas.
intuitive: Replace-all. If something is true for P, and you swap every P with some other formula, the truth is preserved. Like finding-and-replacing every "cat" with "dog" in a sentence — the grammatical structure stays the same.

definition: A name for an individual object in first-order logic. Lowercase letters (x, y, t, a, b…) are term variables. Terms appear as arguments to predicates via `(App P t)`, and are bound by quantifiers in formulas like `(forall x …)`.
intuitive: A name for a specific thing. Like "Alice" or "42" — terms are the objects that predicates describe. In "Alice is tall," Alice is the term.

definition: The $\forall$-intro rule: if you can prove $\varphi$ for an arbitrary variable x (with no other assumptions about x), then $\forall$x.$\varphi$ holds. 'If it works for any x, it works for all x.' Use the `(forall-intro x N)` command — the variable x must not appear free in other assumptions.
intuitive: "If it works for an arbitrary example, it works for everything." Prove something for a generic, unspecified x (with no special assumptions about it), and you've proved it for all x.

definition: Written $\forall$ and typed as `forall`. The formula $\forall$x.$\varphi$ reads 'for all x, $\varphi$ holds'. To prove it, use $\forall$-intro (generalise from an arbitrary variable). To use it, apply $\forall$-elim: from $\forall$x.$\varphi$, you can conclude $\varphi$[t/x] for any term t.
intuitive: "For all" or "for every." Like saying "everyone in this room speaks French" — you're making a claim about every single person, with no exceptions.

definition: A specific term used to instantiate an existential quantifier. In $\exists$-intro, you provide a witness term t: from $\varphi$[t/x], conclude $\exists$x.$\varphi$. In $\exists$-elim, you introduce a fresh witness variable, prove your goal under it, and then discharge it — the witness must not appear free in the final conclusion.
intuitive: A concrete example that proves something exists. If you claim "there is a prime number between 10 and 20," showing the number 13 is your witness — it's the specific thing that makes the claim true.

definition: The syntactic symbol $\vdash$ in a judgement $\Gamma \vdash$ p, read '$\Gamma$ entails p' or 'from $\Gamma$ we prove p'. The turnstile separates the context (assumptions) on the left from the conclusion on the right.
intuitive: A wall dividing what we assume from what we must prove. Everything to the left of $\vdash$ is temporarily borrowed; everything to the right is the claim we are defending.

definition: Applying a function or predicate to an argument to produce a new expression. In Plato, application is written `(App f x)`. Repeated application is used to represent functions and predicates of multiple arguments.
intuitive: Feeding an input into a function. Give the function one argument, and it returns the next result.

definition: Representing a function or predicate of multiple arguments as a sequence of one-argument functions. For example, `Loves(Alice, Bob)` is represented as `(App (App Loves Alice) Bob)`.
intuitive: Instead of giving all the inputs at once, you hand them over one by one. Each step produces something waiting for the next argument.

definition: A symbol representing an arbitrary term. Variables may be free or bound by quantifiers. Lowercase letters such as `x`, `y`, and `z` are commonly used.
intuitive: A placeholder for an object. Like `x` in algebra, it stands for 'some thing' whose identity may not yet be known.

definition: The part of a formula in which a variable or assumption is valid. A quantifier binds variables only within its scope.
intuitive: The region where a name is meaningful. Outside its scope, the variable no longer refers to anything.

definition: A symbol that maps terms to terms. Functions are applied to arguments using application, just like predicates, but produce another term rather than a formula.
intuitive: A machine that takes one object and returns another object.

definition: A symbol denoting a function in first-order logic, such as `succ` or `father`. Applying a function symbol to terms produces another term.
intuitive: The name of a function. Like '+' names addition, `succ` names the successor function.

definition: A symbol that denotes a specific individual object. Constant symbols are terms with no arguments, such as `Socrates` or `0`.
intuitive: A proper name for one particular object.

definition: The collection of predicate symbols, function symbols, and constant symbols available in a logical language, together with their arities.
intuitive: The vocabulary of a logical language—the names you're allowed to use.

definition: The number of arguments that a function or predicate expects. In Plato, even functions of higher arity are represented as nested unary applications through currying.
intuitive: How many inputs something expects before it's complete.

definition: Renaming a bound variable without changing the meaning of a formula. For example, $\forall$x. P(x) and $\forall$y. P(y) are equivalent.
intuitive: Changing the name of a placeholder. Calling it `x` or `y` doesn't matter as long as you're talking about the same thing.

definition: A convenient shorthand notation that does not add expressive power to a language — it desugars into the same underlying form. For example, `(App P a b c)` is sugar for the fully curried `(App (App (App P a) b) c)`.
intuitive: A shorter way to write the same thing. The machine quietly expands it to the real form before any reasoning begins.

definition: A node in a Kripke model representing a complete way things could be. Formulas are evaluated relative to a world.
intuitive: A complete snapshot of reality — one possible version of how things could be.

definition: A structure consisting of a set of worlds, an accessibility relation between worlds, and a valuation function assigning truth to atomic formulas at each world.
intuitive: The full map of all possible worlds and how they connect.

definition: A relation R(w, v) indicating that world v is accessible from world w. Modal operators are interpreted by quantifying over accessible worlds.
intuitive: A rule that says which worlds are reachable from which others — like paths between possible realities.

definition: The modal operator $\Box$P meaning 'P holds in all worlds accessible from the current world'.
intuitive: Something that must be true no matter how things could unfold.

definition: The modal operator $\Diamond$P meaning 'P holds in at least one world accessible from the current world'. Equivalent to $\lnot$\Box$\lnot P$.
intuitive: Something that could be true in at least one alternative version of reality.

definition: A formula is true at a world w in a Kripke model if it satisfies the semantic rules for its structure at that world.
intuitive: Whether a statement holds in a specific possible version of reality.

definition: When evaluating $\Box$P at world w, reasoning temporarily shifts to all worlds v such that R(w, v) holds.
intuitive: Stepping into every possible alternative version of reality to check if something still holds.

definition: If $\vdash$ P (P is provable without assumptions), then $\vdash \Box$P.
intuitive: If something can be proven absolutely, then it is necessary in every possible world.

definition: The weakest normal modal logic containing the K axiom $\Box$($P \to Q$) $\to$ ($\Box$P $\to \Box$Q) and closed under modus ponens and necessitation.
intuitive: The simplest consistent rule system for reasoning about necessity and possibility across worlds.

definition: A semantic framework where modal formulas are evaluated across a structured set of worlds connected by an accessibility relation.
intuitive: Instead of asking 'is this true?', we ask 'in which versions of reality is this true?'

definition: A modal logic containing the K axiom and closed under necessitation and substitution.
intuitive: A modal logic that behaves consistently across all worlds, without breaking structural rules.

definition: The command `(assume F)` introduces a formula F as a temporary hypothesis, yielding {F} $\vdash$ F. It is the starting point of most proofs — a borrowing of truth for the sake of the argument.
intuitive: Put a proposition on the table and say 'let's pretend this is true.' The proof continues under that borrowed belief.

definition: The symbol $\bot$ representing a contradiction — the impossible. If a proof reaches $\bot$, it has arrived at an absurdity. From $\bot$, anything follows (ex falso quodlibet).
intuitive: The point where reasoning breaks reality. If you reach bottom, logic itself has caught fire.

definition: The symbol $\top$ representing truth that requires no proof. Unlike other propositions, $\top$ is an axiom — you may introduce it at any time with `(top-intro)`, regardless of context.
intuitive: The one thing that is always true, no questions asked. Logic's free lunch.

definition: The fragment of logic dealing with atomic propositions and the connectives $\lnot$, $\land$, $\lor$, and $\to$. In propositional logic, you cannot quantify over terms or apply modal operators. It is the simplest and most foundational layer of logical reasoning.
intuitive: Logic that only asks 'is this statement true or false?' — without looking inside the statement or asking in which world.

definition: A property of the accessibility relation R in a Kripke model: every world can reach itself, i.e., R(w, w) for all w. In System K, reflexivity is NOT assumed — R is unconstrained.
intuitive: Every world can see itself. If this holds, then $\Box$$P \to P$ is valid. In System K, we do not assume this.
